summary: My father's family was from Minnesota, and growing up there meant being part of a community with strong traditions. This sense of community deeply influenced my interest in social customs and rituals, which later became central to my anthropological work. Minnesota was where my early appreciation for cultural practices took root.

I attended the University of Minnesota for my undergraduate studies, where I was fortunate to learn from scholars like Albert Jenks. It was there that I began to explore social forces and traditions seriously. Working with Ruth and Wilson Wallis during this time, as well as my summer fieldwork with the Pomo Indians, gave me invaluable practical experience in anthropology.

Albert Jenks was one of my professors at the University of Minnesota who encouraged me to examine social forces and traditions critically. His guidance helped shape my early academic interests in anthropology. Working under his mentorship was an important step in my intellectual development.

Wilson Wallis was a key figure during my time at the University of Minnesota, where I worked closely with him alongside Ruth. Their mentorship was particularly influential in shaping my understanding of social structures. This experience, combined with fieldwork, helped me gain practical insights into cultural practices.

During a summer while I was at the University of Minnesota, I had the opportunity to work with the Pomo Indians in California. This fieldwork gave me practical insights into life histories and cultural practices, which were foundational for my ethnographic approach. The experience deepened my appreciation for indigenous social customs.

Working with the Makah Indians was a formative experience where I learned to engage closely with a community and understand their social structures. This fieldwork taught me the importance of building relationships and respecting the community's way of life. It was a significant step in developing my ethnographic methods.

My time at the Poston War Relocation Camp exposed me to the harsh realities of displacement during World War II. Documenting the stories of those interned there deepened my commitment to social justice and ethnographic fieldwork. This experience was pivotal in shaping my approach to anthropology as a tool for understanding human suffering and resilience.

In 1946, I went to Northern Rhodesia to work with Max Gluckman at the Rhodes-Livingstone Institute. This was an exciting period where I studied social organization and rituals, including work on the rain shrine. The challenging fieldwork there helped me develop a more nuanced anthropological perspective.

Max Gluckman was my mentor at the Rhodes-Livingstone Institute in Northern Rhodesia starting in 1946. Working with him was an exciting and formative experience, as we focused on social organization and rituals. His guidance was crucial in shaping my anthropological approach during that period.

The Rhodes-Livingstone Institute in Northern Rhodesia was where I conducted significant fieldwork beginning in 1946. Under Max Gluckman's leadership, we studied social structures and rituals, including the rain shrine. The institute provided a challenging but rewarding environment that shaped my anthropological perspective.

Starting in 1956, I studied the Gwembe Tonga people who were undergoing forced resettlement due to a dam project. This work involved examining migration patterns, community responses, and the social impact of displacement. It was difficult but important research that offered insights into resilience and adaptation under pressure.

After my fieldwork in Africa, I held a position at Manchester University where I continued my research and teaching in anthropology. This role allowed me to further develop my academic career and share my field experiences with students. It was a valuable phase before moving on to Boston University and later UC Berkeley.

I worked at Boston University following my time at Manchester University, continuing my research and teaching in anthropology. This period helped me expand my academic focus and prepare for my eventual move to UC Berkeley. Boston University was an important step in my professional development.

In 1964, I joined the Department of Anthropology at the University of California, Berkeley, where I became involved in faculty groups, committees, and mentoring students. Berkeley was where I expanded my work to include broader issues in social anthropology and ethics. It was also a place where I engaged actively in advancing the status of women in academia.
